This proposal reveals limited info and keeps our side concealed.

Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are Evil. The proposed team [0,2] includes a known Evil player, so from Good perspective this team is highly likely to fail or at minimum gives Evil control/information. In round 1, rejecting a bad team is optimal and does not reveal much beyond reasonable suspicion.
Player 2: No concrete role information yet from quest outcomes or voting patterns. As a Good Servant on round 1 quest 1, approving a reasonable 2-person team is standard to gather information and avoid unnecessary early rejections. Team [0,2] includes me, which slightly reduces risk from my perspective, and my vote reveals little beyond a cooperative good opening.
Player 0: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate; Player 2 is likely good or unknown. The current phase is team voting on quest 1 for team [0,2], which includes me but not my teammate. As evil, approving a team with me on it gives me a chance to fail the quest immediately and put good on the back foot. Voting approve is also natural for a leader backing their own proposal and reveals little beyond normal self-interest.
Player 1: I know player 0 is my evil teammate, while players 2/3/4 are unknown good roles. The current phase is team voting on quest 1, and the proposed team [0,2] includes one evil (0) and one unknown, which is favorable for evil because we can choose to fail the quest if desired. Approving reveals little beyond normal support for a plausible round-1 team, and getting an evil player onto the first quest helps our side more than rejecting does.
Player 3: No concrete role information yet from quest outcomes or discussion. As a Servant on round 1 quest 1, a 2-player team with the leader included is fairly standard, and approving early teams helps gather information while avoiding unnecessary early rejections. My vote reveals little and approving does not significantly hurt Good here.

Player 0: I am Evil Minion with teammate 1. The proposed team [0,1,2] includes both evil players, which gives us control to fail quest 2 if approved. Prior quest [0,2] succeeded, which makes 0 and 2 look somewhat good; adding 1 may create suspicion later, but for Evil the immediate objective is to get this strong team through. Voting approve aligns with maximizing chance to sabotage while not revealing much, since evil generally wants teams with evil members approved.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are Evil. The proposed team [0,1,2] contains two known evil players, so approving it would strongly risk a failed quest and also gives bad players control. In team voting as Good, especially Merlin, I should reject teams with known evil unless forced by table dynamics. Rejecting reveals some resistance to this bad team, but that is acceptable because preventing an evil-heavy team from going is more important.
Player 3: Quest 1 team [0,2] succeeded, so 0 and 2 are somewhat more trusted though one could still be evil and chose pass. Proposed team adds 1 and excludes me and 4. As a good Servant on quest 2 with a 3-person team, approving a team containing both previously successful members is reasonable; rejecting would give little information. My vote reveals mild trust in 0 and 2 and willingness to test 1.
Player 2: I only know quest 1 team [0,2] succeeded, which makes 0 and 2 somewhat more likely good but does not clear either because evil can pass early. There is little evidence on 1 yet. As a good Servant in team voting, I want to approve reasonable teams that include me and at least one player from a successful prior quest, especially early when information is limited. Approving [0,1,2] keeps 0 and me from the successful team and tests 1, revealing useful information from the quest outcome without being overly risky.
Player 1: I know player 0 is my evil teammate; players 2,3,4 are unknown good/Merlin possibilities. Quest 1 succeeded on team [0,2], which is consistent with 0 choosing to pass to avoid early suspicion. In team voting as evil Assassin, the optimal play is usually to approve a team containing both evil players, since [0,1,2] gives us strong control over quest 2 and a chance to fail while looking somewhat natural because I proposed it. Approving also avoids the inconsistency of rejecting my own team, which would reveal unnecessary information and raise suspicion on me.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are evil. The proposed team [0,2] includes known evil 0, so approving risks another failed quest. As good, optimal play is to reject teams containing a known evil when possible. My vote reveals limited info; rejecting is consistent with a good player and helps avoid a bad team passing.
Player 0: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,1,2] failed with exactly one fail, which implicates either me or Player 1 from my perspective, but others may suspect 0 or 1. Current proposed team [0,2] includes me but excludes my teammate, making it plausible and potentially likely to pass. As evil, I want teams with at least one evil on them approved, especially a 2-person team where I can choose to fail and put evil up 2-1. Voting approve is optimal. My approve is consistent with supporting my own team and does not reveal much beyond that.
Player 3: I know quest 1 with [0,2] succeeded, and quest 2 with [0,1,2] had exactly one fail. That strongly suggests the evil player is either 1 or one of 0/2 who chose not to fail on quest 1, but the simplest read is that adding 1 caused the fail. As a good Servant on a team vote, optimal play is to reject uncertain 2-player teams that still include one of the unresolved pair 0/2 without testing alternatives, especially since quest 3 is pivotal. Approving gives little new info and risks sending an evil player again; rejecting pressures leaders to propose teams that exclude suspicious players and may generate more informative votes.
Player 1: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Team [0,2] previously succeeded with 0 fails, which makes 2 look somewhat good or at least not confirmed evil. My prior team [0,1,2] failed with exactly 1 fail, which implicates either me or 0 to observers; since I am evil, that fits. In team voting as evil, I want outcomes that preserve ambiguity and give evil chances to fail quests later. Approving [0,2] puts my teammate 0 on quest without me, which is generally good because 0 can choose whether to fail based on table state, and this team may be trusted due to prior success. Rejecting would be suspicious given evil often likes to block uncertain teams, and approving helps maintain cover while enabling an evil-controlled quest seat.
Player 2: I am Good (Servant). From the results: Quest 1 with [0,2] succeeded, so both 0 and 2 could be good, though an evil 0 could have passed. Quest 2 with [0,1,2] had exactly 1 fail, and since I know I am good, the fail came from either 0 or 1. That makes player 2 (me) safe and at least one of 0/1 evil. Repeating team [0,2] excludes player 1, so if 1 was the sole evil on Quest 2 this team is clean; if 0 was evil, this team is bad. Given limited info, [0,2] is better than including 1, and approving preserves a tested core with me on it. My vote reveals some confidence in excluding 1 but does not overly help evil. Optimal good play is to approve this relatively strong 2-person team.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin and know players 0 and 1 are evil. Proposed team [1,3,4] includes confirmed evil player 1, so as Good I should reject to avoid likely quest failure. Rejecting also avoids signaling extra information beyond normal resistance to a bad team.
Player 3: Quests 2 and 3 both failed and both included players 0 and 2, while quest 1 with team 0 and 2 succeeded. That strongly suggests 0 and 2 are the two evil players who split pass/fail on quest 1 and then used single fails on quests 2 and 3. As a Good servant on quest 4, optimal play is to reject any team containing 0 or 2 and approve teams excluding both. Proposed team [1,3,4] excludes the strongest evil suspects and includes me, so approving helps Good and reveals that Good is trying to lock in the clean trio.
Player 1: I am evil Assassin with teammate 0. Quests 2 and 3 failed with teams including 0, and quest 3 was [0,2], so 2 is strongly good from our perspective because only 0 could have failed there. Current proposed team [1,3,4] contains me and excludes 0. Since evil already leads 2-1, approving gives me a chance to fail quest 4 directly and win immediately. Revealing support for a team without 0 is acceptable because my fail on the quest secures evil victory if approved.
Player 2: I am Good Servant. Quest 1 [0,2] succeeded, so at most one of 0/2 was evil then, but Quest 2 [0,1,2] failed and Quest 3 [0,2] failed, which strongly implies exactly one of {0,2} is evil and player 1 is likely good (since adding 1 to the successful pair caused a fail only if one of 0/2 was evil already, and Quest 3 confirms one of 0/2 can fail). Team [1,3,4] excludes both suspicious players 0 and 2, so from my perspective it is the best available team to test/secure a success on must-win Quest 4. Approving reveals I support excluding the main suspects, which helps Good more than it helps Evil.
Player 0: I know player 1 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 with [0,1,2] failed, so from public info either 0 or 1 is suspicious; Quest 3 with [0,2] failed makes 0 and/or 2 suspicious, which helps cover 1. Current team [1,3,4] excludes me but includes my teammate 1. Since evil already leads 2-1, approving a team with an evil player gives us a strong chance to secure the fourth quest fail and win immediately. My vote reveals little because evil may plausibly support this team as 1 looks less implicated than I do.
